title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read an article about fashion and identity. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: Wearing Yourself
content.subtitle: What our clothes say about who we are — and who we want to become
content.text:
What we wear has always communicated more than comfort or practicality. Dress is a language, and like all languages, it operates on multiple levels simultaneously — conveying information to others while shaping the wearer's own sense of self. [31]The relationship between clothing and identity is not one-directional: fashion does not merely reflect who we are but actively participates in the construction of who we become.[/31]

Sociologists have long studied the role of dress in identity formation, particularly during adolescence, when the question of self-definition is at its most urgent. [32]The teenager who adopts the dress code of a particular subculture — the specific jacket, the deliberate footwear, the carefully calculated hairstyle — is not simply following fashion but making a claim: this is the kind of person I am, or the kind of person I intend to be.[/32]

This communicative dimension of fashion extends into adult life, though it often becomes less conscious. [33]The professional who wears a suit communicates competence and conformity to institutional expectations; the same person, on a weekend, wearing deliberately casual clothes, signals a different identity — one temporarily freed from professional obligation. The choice to dress down is as deliberate as the choice to dress up.[/33]

Fashion's relationship with identity is not, however, purely a matter of conscious self-expression. The fashion industry itself plays a powerful role in determining which identities are available for adoption at any given moment. [34]Critics argue that what appears to be personal choice is, in reality, heavily shaped by commercial forces — that the 'freedom' of fashion is largely the freedom to choose between options that the industry has already prepared, each carrying its own ideological associations.[/34]

This critique has gained particular traction in discussions of fast fashion, in which clothing is produced rapidly and cheaply, encouraging consumers to update their wardrobes frequently. [35]Proponents of sustainable fashion argue that the pace and disposability of fast fashion are incompatible with thoughtful self-expression: when clothes are treated as throwaway items, the relationship between dress and identity becomes correspondingly shallow.[/35]

[36]Ultimately, what the study of fashion reveals is that clothing is never merely clothing. It is a site where personal desire and social expectation meet, where individual choice and commercial pressure intersect, and where the ongoing human project of self-definition is negotiated — one outfit at a time.[/36]

questions.question: What does the opening paragraph argue about the relationship between fashion and identity?
options: A) That clothing choices are primarily determined by practical rather than social factors.
B) That fashion is a superficial concern when compared with other aspects of identity.
C) That people choose clothes mainly to signal their social status to others.
D) That fashion both reflects and helps to actively create a person's sense of who they are.
questions.explanation: The text states fashion 'does not merely reflect who we are but actively participates in the construction of who we become,' showing a two-way relationship.

questions.question: What point is made about teenagers who adopt subculture dress codes?
options: A) They are expressing and asserting a particular identity through their clothing choices.
B) They are mainly motivated by a desire to rebel against their parents' authority.
C) They are usually unaware of the wider social significance of their dress choices.
D) They tend to abandon these dress codes once they enter the professional world.
questions.explanation: The teenager is described as 'making a claim: this is the kind of person I am, or the kind of person I intend to be,' which is an act of identity assertion.

questions.question: What does the example of the professional's weekend clothes illustrate?
options: A) That adults become progressively less concerned with fashion as they grow older.
B) That formal dress codes are becoming less relevant in modern workplaces.
C) That deliberate choices about dressing casually carry meaning just as formal choices do.
D) That people feel significantly more comfortable in casual clothes than in formal attire.
questions.explanation: The text states that 'the choice to dress down is as deliberate as the choice to dress up,' showing casual dress is equally communicative and intentional.

questions.question: What criticism does the article present about freedom of fashion choice?
options: A) That fashion magazines exert too much influence on young people's self-image.
B) That the options available to consumers are pre-selected and shaped by commercial interests.
C) That people from lower-income groups have less genuine freedom of choice in what they wear.
D) That the industry promotes mass conformity while publicly claiming to celebrate individuality.
questions.explanation: Critics argue the 'freedom' of fashion is 'largely the freedom to choose between options that the industry has already prepared,' limiting genuine choice.

questions.question: According to advocates of sustainable fashion, what is wrong with fast fashion?
options: A) It causes significant environmental damage through the widespread use of synthetic materials.
B) It produces clothing of such poor quality that it cannot survive more than a few washes.
C) It makes fashionable clothing impossible to afford for consumers on low incomes.
D) It reduces clothing to something disposable, undermining any meaningful link to identity.
questions.explanation: Sustainable fashion advocates argue that when clothes are 'treated as throwaway items, the relationship between dress and identity becomes correspondingly shallow.'

questions.question: What does the final paragraph suggest about the significance of clothing?
options: A) Clothes are a space where personal, social, and commercial influences all converge.
B) Fashion is ultimately more about commerce and economics than about personal expression.
C) The study of fashion provides insights mainly into the workings of consumer society.
D) Individual clothing choices are too complex to be meaningfully understood through analysis.
questions.explanation: The final paragraph describes clothing as a site where 'personal desire and social expectation meet' and where 'individual choice and commercial pressure intersect.'
